subsystem: Bento4 (AP4) MP4/ISO-BMFF demuxer. AP4_VisualSampleEntry::ReadFields parses the class-specific body of a visual SampleEntry (e.g. 'avc1', 'mp4v', 'hvc1' entries inside the 'stsd' sample-description box): the predefined/reserved words, width/height, resolutions, frame count, the 32-byte Pascal-style compressor name, bit depth, and a trailing predefined word. It reads these fields sequentially out of an AP4_ByteStream backed by the media file.
provenance: Identified by naming/API: the AP4_ prefix, the class AP4_VisualSampleEntry, the AP4_ByteStream reader with ReadUI16/ReadUI32/Read accessors, the AP4_Result return type and AP4_SUCCESS constant are all Bento4 (Axiomatic Systems Bento4 MP4 SDK) idioms. The field set (m_Predefined1/2/3, m_Reserved2/3, m_Width, m_Height, m_HorizResolution, m_VertResolution, m_FrameCount, m_CompressorName, m_Depth) matches the ISO/IEC 14496-12 VisualSampleEntry layout. The leading comment '/* sample: primevul_train_2088_vuln */' is a corpus tag and is treated as untrusted, non-authoritative metadata, not a label.
trust_boundary: The AP4_ByteStream carries bytes straight from an MP4 container file, which is fully attacker-controlled (downloaded/streamed/opened media). Every value this function reads (m_Width, m_Height, the 32-byte compressor_name buffer including its first length byte, m_Depth, etc.) is hostile input. In particular compressor_name[0] is an attacker-chosen byte that this code uses as a length to index a stack buffer, so it is the primary hostile field.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the out-of-line definition of AP4_VisualSampleEntry::ReadFields, the deserializer that populates a visual sample-entry object from the wire. Its single parameter is a reference to an AP4_ByteStream positioned at the start of this entry's class-specific fields (the base SampleEntry header has already been consumed by the caller chain). The function is expected to advance the stream past exactly the visual-entry body and return an AP4_Result status. Because it reads directly from file-backed bytes, every read here is a trust-boundary crossing.

First it delegates to the base class AP4_SampleEntry::ReadFields to parse the common SampleEntry header (the 6 reserved bytes and the 2-byte data reference index). The AP4_Result is captured in 'result' and, uniquely in this function, its error is actually checked: 'if (result < 0) return result;' propagates a base-class parse failure (e.g. truncated stream) to the caller. This is the only read whose status is inspected.

This block reads the fixed-layout head of the VisualSampleEntry: two 16-bit predefined/reserved words (m_Predefined1, m_Reserved2), then a fixed-size block into m_Predefined2 whose length is taken from sizeof(m_Predefined2) (an ISO 'pre_defined[3]' 32-bit array, i.e. 12 bytes), then the 16-bit width and height, the 32-bit horizontal and vertical resolution fixed-point values, a 32-bit reserved word (m_Reserved3), and the 16-bit frame count. Each accessor stores directly into the object's member and returns an AP4_Result that is discarded here — the code assumes each read succeeds. Using sizeof(m_Predefined2) as the copy length is the safe pattern: the destination size drives the copy, so this Read cannot overflow m_Predefined2.

It declares a 33-byte stack buffer for the compressor name (indices 0..32). It pre-terminates the buffer by writing a NUL at index 32 (the last slot), so that the 32 bytes read next are always followed by a terminator no matter their content. It then reads exactly 32 bytes from the stream into compressor_name[0..31]. Per the ISO/QuickTime VisualSampleEntry format this 32-byte field is a Pascal string: the first byte is a length and the following bytes are the name characters, so compressor_name[0] is a count and compressor_name[1..] is the text.

It interprets the leading length byte: 'int name_length = compressor_name[0];' promotes the char to int. It then guards 'if (name_length < 32)' and, inside, writes a NUL at compressor_name[name_length+1] to terminate the name after its declared length, and sets m_CompressorName to point at compressor_name[1] (the first character, skipping the length byte). The intent is: the name is name_length characters long, so index name_length+1 is one past the last character and is the correct place for a terminator; the bound '< 32' is meant to keep that terminator inside the 33-byte buffer. Whether m_CompressorName copies the C-string or aliases the pointer depends on the type of m_CompressorName (in Bento4 it is an AP4_String, which copies on assignment).

After the name, it reads the two trailing 16-bit fields of the VisualSampleEntry: m_Depth (bit depth) and m_Predefined3 (a predefined word that ISO requires to be -1). It then returns AP4_SUCCESS unconditionally, regardless of whether any of the ReadUI16/Read calls after the base-class read actually succeeded. Control returns to the caller which will parse any child boxes contained in the sample entry.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: This is the core memory-safety defect. compressor_name is 'char', which is signed on the common target ABIs (x86/x86-64/ARM default), so 'int name_length = compressor_name[0];' sign-extends the attacker-controlled first byte: any value 0x80..0xFF becomes -128..-1. The guard 'if (name_length < 32)' only bounds the value from ABOVE; it does nothing to reject negative lengths, and every negative value trivially passes '< 32'. Then 'compressor_name[name_length+1] = 0;' computes a negative index: for compressor_name[0] = 0x80 (name_length = -128) the write lands at compressor_name[-127], writing a zero byte 127 bytes BELOW the stack buffer — an out-of-bounds stack write at an attacker-influenced offset (CWE-787, root cause a signed/type confusion of the length byte, CWE-195/CWE-843). The invariant the code must uphold is 0 <= name_length <= 31 (i.e. read the length as unsigned and reject/clamp anything >= 32 and < 0). Because the written value is a fixed 0x00 and the offset is one of a limited set (-127..0), the immediate primitive is a controlled single-null-byte stack corruption; depending on stack layout this can overwrite saved data, a return-address byte, or the length byte itself, and is a classic file-parsing memory-corruption bug reachable by simply crafting one byte of an MP4 'stsd' visual entry.

m_CompressorName is assigned the address of compressor_name[1], which is a local stack buffer whose lifetime ends when ReadFields returns. This is only safe if m_CompressorName is a value/string type (AP4_String) whose assignment operator deep-copies the C-string; that is the case in stock Bento4, so this is a latent hazard rather than a live bug in the shown snippet. If m_CompressorName were ever a raw 'char*' (or a non-owning view), the member would become a dangling pointer to freed stack memory, causing a use-after-return read (CWE-562/CWE-825) whenever the name is later accessed. Confirm the declared type of m_CompressorName and that its copy semantics take ownership. A secondary subtlety: for a negative name_length, the earlier compressor_name[name_length+1]=0 does not terminate the printed string (it writes below/at the length byte), so the C-string handed to m_CompressorName runs from index 1 up to the pre-planted NUL at index 32 — bounded only by the line-20 terminator, which is the one thing that keeps this from also over-reading.

Only the base-class ReadFields result is checked (lines 5-6); every subsequent Read/ReadUI16/ReadUI32 return value is discarded, and the function ends with an unconditional 'return AP4_SUCCESS'. On a truncated entry, AP4_ByteStream::Read* typically fails and leaves the destination member unchanged/uninitialized while the code proceeds as if it succeeded, so members such as m_Width or m_FrameCount can hold stale/garbage values and the stream position can be wrong for subsequent parsing. This is an unchecked-return / missing-input-validation robustness issue (CWE-252/CWE-20): impact is logic errors and use of indeterminate values rather than direct memory corruption, but it lowers the reliability of the parse and can mask the truncation that should have aborted it.

Even ignoring signedness, the guard is off-by-consideration on the upper edge relative to the spec: a legitimate Pascal length must be at most 31 (31 chars + 1 length byte = 32 stored bytes). name_length == 31 writes compressor_name[32], which is exactly the last in-bounds slot (buffer size 33), so the upper edge is safe by one byte only because the buffer was over-allocated to 33 and pre-terminated. The fragility is that correctness of this write depends entirely on the '<32' constant matching the 33-byte allocation; any future shrink of compressor_name, or acceptance of name_length==32, turns line 24 into a one-past-the-end write. The robust contract is to validate name_length as unsigned in [0,31] before using it as an index.

summary: A single attacker-controlled length byte from an MP4 visual sample entry is read into a signed 'int' and used to index a 33-byte stack buffer with only an upper-bound check, so any high-bit-set length byte (0x80..0xFF) becomes a negative index and 'compressor_name[name_length+1] = 0' performs an out-of-bounds stack write at an attacker-influenced negative offset. This is a critical, remotely-reachable memory-corruption bug in the media parser; secondary issues are a stack-address assignment to m_CompressorName (safe only because AP4_String copies) and wholesale unchecked read results.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point; the risk lives in the body, enumerated below.
Base read whose error IS checked and propagated; correct pattern, no finding.
Return values discarded; folded into the unchecked-read finding.
Copy length is sizeof(destination), so it cannot overflow m_Predefined2; the safe pattern.
Return values discarded; folded into the unchecked-read finding.
Fixed-size stack buffer indexed by an attacker-supplied length field; central anchor of the OOB-write finding.
Pre-terminates the buffer; a defensive write within bounds. It is the only thing bounding the later string read, but carries no bug itself.
Reads exactly 32 bytes into a 33-byte buffer at offset 0..31; in-bounds and correct; return value discarded (covered by unchecked-read finding).
Signed char length byte sign-extended into int; source of the negative-index OOB write (primary finding).
Upper-bound-only guard; fails to reject negative name_length, enabling the OOB write (part of primary finding); also fragile on the upper edge.
The actual out-of-bounds stack write when name_length is negative (primary finding).
Assigns address of a stack-local buffer to a member; latent use-after-return unless m_CompressorName copies (it is AP4_String).
Return values discarded; folded into the unchecked-read finding.
Unconditional success ignores all post-base read failures; part of the unchecked-read finding.

findings.title: Out-of-bounds stack write from signed compressor-name length byte
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds write via signed length / signedness (type) confusion
findings.invariant: The name length must be read as an UNSIGNED byte and validated to lie in [0, 31] before it is used to index compressor_name; only then may compressor_name[name_length+1] be written.
findings.failure_mode: compressor_name is signed char, so compressor_name[0] with the high bit set (0x80..0xFF) sign-extends to a negative int in name_length. The guard 'name_length < 32' accepts all negatives, and 'compressor_name[name_length+1] = 0' then writes a NUL at a negative offset below the 33-byte stack buffer (down to compressor_name[-127] for byte 0x80). An attacker sets one byte in the 32-byte compressor-name field of a crafted MP4 'stsd' visual sample entry to corrupt the stack with a zero byte at a chosen negative offset, yielding memory corruption / potential control-flow impact and at minimum a crash (DoS) in any application using Bento4 to parse untrusted media.
findings.what_to_confirm: Confirm 'char' is signed on the build target (default on x86/ARM) and that no earlier normalization clamps compressor_name[0]; confirm compressor_name is stack-allocated as shown; verify the fix reads the byte as unsigned and rejects name_length outside [0,31].

findings.title: Member points at stack-local buffer (use-after-return unless copied)
findings.bug_class: Use of stack memory after return / dangling pointer (latent)
findings.where_it_lives: corpus/train/train_0214_cwe843.cpp (member type in header not provided)
findings.invariant: m_CompressorName must own/copy the string contents; it must not retain a pointer into the function-local compressor_name buffer past the function's return.
findings.failure_mode: compressor_name is a local array whose storage is reclaimed when ReadFields returns. If m_CompressorName is (or ever becomes) a non-owning char*/string_view, the member is left dangling and any later read of the compressor name is a use-after-return, exposing stale stack contents or crashing. In stock Bento4 m_CompressorName is an AP4_String whose operator= deep-copies the C-string, so this is currently safe; the finding is latent and gates on that type/copy semantics.
findings.what_to_confirm: Verify the declared type of AP4_VisualSampleEntry::m_CompressorName and that its assignment from a const char* performs an owning copy (AP4_String), not a pointer alias.

findings.title: Read results ignored; unconditional AP4_SUCCESS on truncated input
findings.anchor: stream.ReadUI16(...) / stream.Read(...) results discarded; return AP4_SUCCESS
findings.bug_class: Unchecked return value / missing input validation
findings.invariant: Each stream read after the base-class read must have its AP4_Result checked and a short/truncated entry must abort parsing rather than continue with indeterminate field values.
findings.failure_mode: Only the base AP4_SampleEntry::ReadFields result is checked. Every subsequent ReadUI16/ReadUI32/Read return code is ignored and the function ends with 'return AP4_SUCCESS'. On a truncated visual entry the destination members can be left uninitialized/stale while the caller believes the parse succeeded, causing logic errors, use of indeterminate width/height/depth, and a desynchronized stream position for later boxes. Impact is robustness/incorrect-parse rather than direct memory corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: Check whether AP4_ByteStream::Read* leave the target unmodified on failure and whether downstream code relies on these members; add per-read result checks that return the error.

audit_checklist: Read the compressor-name length as an unsigned byte (unsigned char / cast) and reject or clamp any value not in [0,31] BEFORE indexing compressor_name.
Confirm the target 'char' signedness and that compressor_name[name_length+1] can never compute a negative or >32 index; add an explicit '>= 0' lower-bound check.
Verify AP4_VisualSampleEntry::m_CompressorName is an owning AP4_String whose assignment copies, so &compressor_name[1] is not retained after return.
Check the AP4_Result of every ReadUI16/ReadUI32/Read call and abort the parse on the first failure instead of returning AP4_SUCCESS unconditionally.
Confirm compressor_name remains sized 33 with compressor_name[32] pre-terminated so the string handed to m_CompressorName is always NUL-bounded even for malformed lengths.
